title: Sort Variations: Descending Order & Sort + Search Combined
description: Modify bubble sort to sort in descending order, and combine sorting with searching to search a sorted array more efficiently.
congratsText: Great work! You now know how to sort descending and combine sorting with searching.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Modify bubble sort to sort in descending order
  • Combine sorting and searching in one pipeline
  • Explain why searching a sorted array can stop early
  • Recognize how one symbol change flips a sort's direction

sections.title: Tallest to Shortest

quiz.question: If a line sorted shortest-to-tallest needs to become tallest-to-shortest, what actually needs to change?
options: Just the comparison rule used when deciding who swaps with whom
The entire sorting process must be replaced with a different algorithm
Every person needs to be re-measured with a different unit
Nothing — descending order isn't possible with bubble sort

sections.lesson:
Tallest to Shortest
If a line sorted shortest-to-tallest instead needed to go tallest-to-shortest, the underlying process wouldn't change at all — only the comparison rule: swap if this one is shorter, instead of taller.

sections.title: Flipping the Comparison

quiz.question: What single change turns an ascending bubble sort into a descending one?
options: Flipping the comparison operator from > to <
Reversing the array's contents before sorting begins
Changing the outer loop to count downward instead of upward
Adding a second temp variable to the swap logic

sections.lesson:
Flipping the Comparison
Ascending order swaps when the left neighbor is bigger (arr[j] > arr[j+1]). Descending order swaps when the left neighbor is smaller (arr[j] < arr[j+1]). Everything else about the algorithm — the nested loops, the temp swap — stays exactly the same.

sections.title: Implementing Descending Bubble Sort

quiz.question: What does this loop produce for arr = {4, 1, 3, 2}?

sections.lesson:
Implementing Descending Bubble Sort
With the comparison flipped to <, larger values now 'win' each swap and move toward the front, producing a largest-to-smallest order.
for (int i = 0; i < size - 1; i++) { for (int j = 0; j < size - i - 1; j++) { if (arr[j] < arr[j + 1]) { int temp = arr[j]; arr[j] = arr[j + 1]; arr[j + 1] = temp; } } } // {4, 1, 3, 2} becomes {4, 3, 2, 1}

sections.title: Why Search a Sorted Array Differently

quiz.question: Why can searching a sorted, ascending array sometimes stop before reaching the end?
options: Once the current element exceeds the target, nothing further in the array can match
Sorted arrays are always shorter than unsorted arrays
Every value in a sorted array is guaranteed to be unique
It can't — sorted and unsorted arrays are always searched identically

sections.lesson:
Why Search a Sorted Array Differently
In an ascending sorted array, once the loop reaches a value larger than the target, every remaining value must also be larger — so the target cannot possibly appear later. The search can safely stop early instead of scanning to the end.

sections.title: Implementing an Early-Stop Sorted Search

quiz.question: In this sorted search, what causes the loop to stop early?
options: Finding a value greater than the target, since nothing after it can match
Finding a value equal to the array's size
Reaching exactly the middle index of the array
The loop never stops early — it always checks every element

sections.lesson:
Implementing an Early-Stop Sorted Search
The extra check — if the current value already exceeds the target, return -1 immediately — is what takes advantage of the sorted order.
int sortedSearch(int arr[], int size, int target) { for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] == target) return i; if (arr[i] > target) return -1; // no point continuing } return -1; }

sections.title: Building a Sort + Search Pipeline

quiz.question: In a sort-then-search pipeline, why must sorting happen BEFORE the early-stop search runs?
options: The early-stop logic only works correctly on data that is already in order
C requires every array to be sorted before it can be declared
Searching always modifies the array, so sorting after would be pointless
It doesn't matter — the order of the two steps has no real effect

sections.lesson:
Building a Sort + Search Pipeline
A pipeline reads an array, sorts it, then searches it — in that order. Running sortedSearch() on unsorted data would be wrong, since the 'stop once you pass the target' logic assumes the values are already increasing.
bubbleSort(arr, size); // step 1: sort int idx = sortedSearch(arr, size, target); // step 2: search

sections.title: Complete Example: Read, Sort, Search, Report

quiz.question: What does this program print if 7 is not present after sorting?
options: Not found
Found at index 7
Found at index -1
The array prints in reverse order

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: Read, Sort, Search, Report
A complete pipeline chains each piece together: read the data, sort it, search it, then report the result clearly — the same reporting pattern used with plain linear search.
bubbleSort(arr, size); int idx = sortedSearch(arr, size, 7); if (idx != -1) { printf("Found at index %d", idx); } else { printf("Not found"); }

sections.title: Best Practice: Sort in Place vs. Keep a Copy

quiz.question: Why might a program want to keep an unsorted copy of the original array before sorting it in place?
options: The original input order may still be needed elsewhere in the program
C automatically deletes any array once it has been sorted
Sorting always changes the array's size, so a copy prevents that
It has no real benefit and is never done in practice

sections.lesson:
Best Practice: Sort in Place vs. Keep a Copy
Because bubble sort modifies the array directly (in place), the original input order is lost once sorting runs. If the original order might still be needed — for display, for undo, or for comparison — copy the array into a second one before sorting.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What is the shared idea connecting descending sort and sorted-array search?
options: Both adapt an existing pattern by changing one comparison, not the overall structure
Both require rewriting the algorithm completely from scratch
Both only work correctly on arrays smaller than 10 elements
Neither actually depends on how the array's elements are ordered

sections.lesson:
Recap
Descending sort flips one comparison symbol in bubble sort. Sorted search adds one extra check that lets it stop early once it passes where the target would be. Neither needed a new algorithm — both reused an existing pattern with a small, deliberate change.
// Descending sort: if (arr[j] < arr[j+1]) swap // Early-stop search: if (arr[i] > target) return -1;
